title: Counting Related Rows Without Inflation
summary: Learn to count matched rows and unique entities accurately in SQL without allowing join multiplication to distort reports.

sketchBlocks.title: Understanding COUNT with Joins
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When working with SQL joins, it's crucial to understand how to count rows accurately without inflating results. Consider a LEFT JOIN between `departments` and `courses` to list all departments and their courses. If a department has no courses, the join will produce NULLs for course columns. To count courses per department without inflating numbers, use `COUNT(courses.id)`. This counts only non-NULL course IDs, ensuring departments with no courses show a count of zero.

```sql
SELECT departments.name, COUNT(courses.id) AS course_count
FROM departments
LEFT JOIN courses ON departments.id = courses.department_id
GROUP BY departments.name;
```

This query lists each department with the number of courses it offers, accurately reflecting zero for departments without courses.

sketchBlocks.title: Using COUNT(DISTINCT) for Unique Counts
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
In SQL, `COUNT(DISTINCT column)` is used to count unique values in a column. This is particularly useful when you want to count unique entities, such as students enrolled in courses. Consider counting unique students per department:

```sql
SELECT departments.name, COUNT(DISTINCT enrollments.student_id) AS unique_students
FROM departments
LEFT JOIN courses ON departments.id = courses.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments ON courses.id = enrollments.course_id
GROUP BY departments.name;
```

This query ensures that each student is counted only once per department, providing an accurate count of unique students enrolled in courses offered by each department.

quizDraft.title: Count Courses per Department
quizDraft.prompt: Write a SQL query to list each department and the number of courses it offers. Use a LEFT JOIN to ensure all departments are included, even those without courses.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
SELECT departments.name, COUNT(courses.id) AS course_count
FROM departments
LEFT JOIN courses ON departments.id = courses.department_id
GROUP BY departments.name;

quizDraft.title: Count Unique Students per Department
quizDraft.prompt: Write a SQL query to list each department and the number of unique students enrolled in its courses. Use COUNT(DISTINCT) to ensure each student is counted only once per department.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
SELECT departments.name, COUNT(DISTINCT enrollments.student_id) AS unique_students
FROM departments
LEFT JOIN courses ON departments.id = courses.department_id
LEFT JOIN enrollments ON courses.id = enrollments.course_id
GROUP BY departments.name;

quizDraft.title: Purpose of COUNT with LEFT JOIN
quizDraft.prompt: What is the purpose of using COUNT(column) with a LEFT JOIN?
options: To count all rows, including NULLs
quizDraft.hint: Consider how COUNT(column) handles NULL values.

help.concept: COUNT(column) with LEFT JOIN counts non-NULL values, preserving unmatched rows.
help.hint_1: COUNT(column) ignores NULLs, which are common in LEFT JOIN results.
help.hint_2: Using COUNT(column) ensures unmatched rows are counted as zero.

quizDraft.title: Benefits of COUNT(DISTINCT)
quizDraft.prompt: What are the benefits of using COUNT(DISTINCT column) in SQL? Select all that apply.
quizDraft.hint: Think about how COUNT(DISTINCT) affects duplicate values.

help.concept: COUNT(DISTINCT) counts unique values, reducing duplicates.
help.hint_1: It helps in counting unique entities in a dataset.
help.hint_2: It prevents duplicate values from inflating counts.

quizDraft.title: COUNT with NULL Handling
quizDraft.prompt: In a LEFT JOIN, using COUNT(column) will count only the [blank1] values.
quizDraft.template: In a LEFT JOIN, using COUNT(column) will count only the [blank1] values.
quizDraft.hint: Consider what COUNT(column) does with NULLs.

help.concept: COUNT(column) counts the missing term values in a result set.
help.hint_1: COUNT(column) ignores NULLs, focusing on actual data.
help.hint_2: NULLs are not counted by COUNT(column).

quizDraft.title: Purpose of COUNT(DISTINCT)
quizDraft.prompt: The purpose of COUNT(DISTINCT column) is to count [blank1] values.
quizDraft.template: The purpose of COUNT(DISTINCT column) is to count [blank1] values.
quizDraft.hint: Think about how COUNT(DISTINCT) handles duplicates.

help.concept: COUNT(DISTINCT) counts the missing term values, excluding duplicates.
help.hint_1: It focuses on the missing term entries in a dataset.
help.hint_2: Duplicates are not counted by COUNT(DISTINCT).

quizDraft.title: Understanding Report Grain
quizDraft.prompt: Why is it important to state the grouped result grain before choosing a count expression? Select all that apply.
options: To define the level of detail
quizDraft.hint: Consider how the result grain affects the interpretation of counts.

help.concept: The result grain defines what each row represents, affecting count accuracy.
help.hint_1: The grain determines the level of detail in the report.
help.hint_2: Choosing the wrong grain can lead to misleading counts.
